Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Level 1 maze. Player is a white cross at (32, 21) inside a gray (G) walkable area. Dark gray (c) is an obstacle wall at cols 29-33, rows 30-39. Target appears to be orange (O) and blue (b) tiles at rows 45-49, cols 34-38. A black box at top shows a blue L-shape pattern (possibly a goal indicator). Yellow bar at bottom is likely a timer.
- Plan: Navigate the player around the c obstacle by going UP above it, RIGHT past it, then DOWN to the colored tiles. First test with one move.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check player position after UP move
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
for r in range(28, 36):
    row_str = lines[r][18:26]
    print(f"Row {r}: {row_str}")

# Also check the yellow bar at bottom
print("\nYellow bar area:")
for r in range(60, 64):
    row_str = lines[r][12:64]
    print(f"Row {r}: {row_str}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Wait, the player hasn't moved! The white cross is still at the same position (rows 31-33, cols 20-22). The UP action didn't move the player. Let me check if the player is blocked or if the movement works differently.
Looking at the original position:
- Row 31: GGGWGGGG (W at col 21)
- Row 32: GGwWWGGG (w at col 20, W at cols 21-22)
- Row 33: GGGwGGGG (w at col 21)
After UP, it's still the same. This means either:
1. The player can't move UP (blocked by something)
2. The movement doesn't work as I expected
Wait - looking more carefully at the yellow bar, it seems to have changed. In the first frame, the yellow bar was longer. Let me compare... Actually, looking at the two images provided, the yellow bar appears shorter in step 1. So the timer IS counting down, but the player didn't move.
Let me check if the player is blocked. Looking at the maze:
- Row 30: GGGGGGGGGGGGGGGcccccGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGG
- Row 31: GGGGGGGWGGGGGGGcccccGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGG
The player is at (31-32, 21). Looking at row 30, col 21 is G. So there should be space to move up.
Wait, let me re-examine. The player's cross shape spans:
- Center W at (32, 21)
- W at (31, 21) - top
- W at (32, 22) - right  
- w at (32, 20) - left
- w at (33, 21) - bottom
If UP moves the player up by 1, the center would go from (32, 21) to (31, 21). But (31, 21) is already part of the player's shape! Maybe the player can't move into its own body?
Actually wait - I need to look more carefully. The player didn't move at all. Let me check if there's a wall above. Looking at row 30, col 21 is G (gray), which should be walkable.
Hmm, but maybe the player's cross shape is 5 pixels, and when moving, the ENTIRE cross needs to fit in walkable space. Let me check if row 30 has G at the right columns.
Row 30: GGGGGGGGGGGGGGGcccccGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGG
Col 14-28 are G, col 29-33 are c.
The player cross at center (32, 21) spans:
- (31, 21), (32, 20), (32, 21), (32, 22), (33, 21)
If moving UP, the new center would be (31, 21), and the cross would span:
- (30, 21), (31, 20), (31, 21), (31, 22), (32, 21)
Row 30, col 21 is G - should be walkable. So why didn't the player move?
Wait, maybe I need to look at the actual grid more carefully. Let me check if the player is actually in a confined space.
Actually, looking at the segmentation again:
- id=7 (W, 3 pixels) boundary: [[31, 21], [32, 22], [32, 21]]
- id=8 (w, 1 pixel) boundary: [[32, 20]]
- id=9 (w, 1 pixel) boundary: [[33, 21]]
So the player cross is:
- (31, 21) - W
- (32, 20) - w
- (32, 21) - W (center)
- (32, 22) - W
- (33, 21) - w
These are all children of id=2 (the G area). The player is inside the G area.
But the player didn't move when I pressed UP. Let me check if the yellow bar changed - if it did, then the action was registered but the player was blocked.
Actually, looking at the two images more carefully - the yellow bar DOES appear shorter in step 1. So the timer is counting down but the player didn't move. This could mean:
1. The player is blocked from moving UP
2. The movement system works differently than I think
Let me try other directions to see which ones work.

sections.content: World model: The UP action didn't move the player - the cross is still at the same position. The yellow bar (timer) decreased, confirming the action was registered. The player may be blocked or movement works differently. Need to test other directions.
